preamble: L-vocalization is a common phonetic process where the alveolar lateral approximant /l/, particularly its velarized allophone [ɫ], loses its consonantal articulation and becomes a vowel or a semivowel. In certain dialects of English, this vocalization can lead to the formation of the diphthong /ɔɪ/.

examples.note: The word 'hole' pronounced with the diphthong [ɔɪ] as 'hoil'.
The word 'coal' pronounced with the diphthong [ɔɪ] as 'coil'.

sources: Sánchez García, J. (2003). A Synchronic Study of the Phonological Variants within the GOAT Lexical Set in the Dialect of Nineteenth-Century Lancashire.
Wright, J. (1898-1905). The English Dialect Dictionary.
